speaker: interviewer
text: Godel Silber. And this is tape number four. The day of liberation. Can you talk about that? Do you know the date of the liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, what do you want to know?

speaker: interviewer
text: What day were you liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: May-- May the 5th.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody knew it. Nobody believed it. Who would believe it? It's not such a thing to believe. If you believe in faith, yes. We didn't know it. People got crazy right away. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Right away, crazy. I mean, crazy, crazy. As a matter of fact, I got a friend from my city-- her brother lives in Detroit. She got right away so crazy. She still is left in Europe. She is in Austria in a hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any warning that they were coming to-- to liberate you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we saw the leaflets. We saw the little papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: They came-- Were you allowed read them? Did anyone read them anyway?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we catched them anyway-- anyway, not to bother anybody. 90% didn't-- were not interested in it. What-- what's it to him anymore? He's dead. He's finished. He doesn't know what's gonna happen in a few minutes. He can-- hardly can walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came, the Americans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- see, I told you. I-- I ranned up on-- in a-- in a big tank like a supply tank for water. And I lay over there. I lay-- we lay a few people over there. At that, I lay only-- and my brother, and fellow from [INAUDIBLE], and another fellow. The [INAUDIBLE] was some more. We were very scared because it was coke, you know, not coal, coke. Hard-- hard coal-- you know what that means-- for the factories-- for the steel companies. So hard coal-- and that was soot-- the soot come right up to the top. But I lay here. I can breathe and I had already-- I saw [INAUDIBLE] as far as a new car-- a car I never saw before-- I know cars. I saw Opels. I saw Chevys. I saw Fords. I saw diesels, Mercedes, but I never saw a car like that-- a short car like the-- the Jeeps. So there's got to be something foreign and in a different [INAUDIBLE] too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were up high. So-- I was the youngest [? one. ?] I let myself down to the pipe. I go to the mirror to take a look. I mean, there was a mirror in the restroom of the [INAUDIBLE], which I'm a black man, a nigger, can't see nothing. You know. I was laying over there a couple days from the-- from the soot. I got black. You know. Finally, I wash myself-- a little on my mouth and everything. And I ran out. So I saw the man. In the meantime, this I never can forget either-- I saw saw the Russian right away, the Ukrainian, right away. He had guns. And he had everything right away. He come to the trials. We should go with them together. Who wants to go with them together and [INAUDIBLE]? So the American come. He said, we shouldn't stay here. We should go back. The-- the-- right here is the hardest spot. If the-- if the-- if the Germans gonna-- don't want it retreat, they are going to start to shoot from here. So they are going to shoot. And they are going to shoot. And we were in the middle. So we went. But we asked him where we should go. He said, the best thing is to go to the Red Cross. Go back a little. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He said, we go back. So we went back a little bit. We stayed over there two days. And after the two days, very good to stay over there. I said we have to go wait. We go to Israel. So how do we go to Israel? So we went into-- to a big farm-- big farm with lots of horses and [? everything. ?] And we went in with him. And we said, listen, we need a horse. We got here a bunch of people. They are hungry. We had to go to the city and collect some food. So he didn't want [INAUDIBLE] and wanted to give us the horse. So that fellow from-- from [INAUDIBLE] [? Germany, ?] no. What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Rosenzweig. ?] So he went. I said, listen, another fellow-- one fellow we had he could talk French. He could talk English, Hebrew. He was before the war in Israel to-- and a  little bit Arabic. He was from Sosnowiec-- very intelligent-- Morris [? Sandler. ?] So finally, that-- that farmer gave us a horse and said, no, I can't give you a horse. We give you a horse with a man. It was still better.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he gave us a man, and we went to nearest city. And the biggest problem was the bridges-- it was the bridges were broke, you know, bombed. So the American in no time made new bridges. So, OK, who is gonna sit on the wagon? While at that time, I was sick already. I had diarrhea very bad. So I couldn't even go down. So that [? Rosenzweig ?] went down I remember like that and keep the horse. The horse shouldn't jump over-- keep the horse. He went over the bridge. He come to the little city-- lots of people. What city was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think [? Buvberg ?] like that-- [? Buvberg. ?] There was nothing there. It was a little city. But the train stopped. You know. People went already. Some went marched-- they marched. Some went by train-- the train station up there. So the-- so the people ran-- ran over there and [INAUDIBLE] because the Americans told-- told them that you can do three days anything you want. We didn't do nothing, not even that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take food? Clothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing. We didn't even-- it didn't even apply. We didn't want to do anything. So listen, two-- one wrong doesn't make two rights, even with the Germans. We didn't do nothing. If there would have been [INAUDIBLE]. It would be how do you call? An order to do that to the Germans. I would be the first to do it, like [INAUDIBLE]. I couldn't do the job that time. Better like the Germans. But you can take-- to [? burn, ?] yes, better than them. I know all the-- all the tricks I couldn't do at that time. I was 20-odd years old. I could do better than them.

speaker: interviewee
text: But listen, no, if I had done it, I would never do  it. Before, yes, I would do it. And all of a sudden, you see some-- another fellow saying, hi, Godel, what are you doing here? I said, nothing. He said, why don't you come and take some shoes-- some bread like that for the fellow, the farmer would send with us. A fellow said, listen, what's going on? Take a look. I got away. I got a [INAUDIBLE]. You know what a [INAUDIBLE] is? No.

speaker: interviewee
text: A [INAUDIBLE] is you got a card like here they give you the card for bread, for clothes. I got a card already two years, and I can't get some shoes. Take a look at people with two, three pairs of shoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So [? Rosenzweig ?] told him-- he was older-- elder [INAUDIBLE] than us-- he said, why don't you go get it? We wait for you here. He said, OK, he waited right in that corner. So when he went down, and he went to the-- to another block of our roadside to the horse and wagon. And we were on the way. That was-- [LAUGHS] That was the only thing that we did. But where are we gong to go? So we go to Switzerland. But what good is it? We have to go through borders. Come to the borders, we had to [INAUDIBLE] for who knows what. We were at the French-- French-- French border. He took away the horse from us. And he gave it away to a German. The horse was so smart, he didn't want to go over there in these hills. Have you ever been to Germany? Hills-- the horse didn't want to go. So about that time the Germans looked around, the French looked around. But we got what happened? So the numbers, they didn't care for nothing. He said, you can't do that. Where'd you get the horse? And we told him. You can't do that. So we-- they let us out. So we told him. You can't arrest us. Listen. We are going to report that. So he let us out and far away with-- with family, a German family with children. The horse stayed. So we went. He took away the horse. And we went with that horse as far to Austria. No, that's right Austria-- Germany. Austria-- Austria got parts like here-- Ontario, Quebec. You know. We went as far as [? Fribourg. ?] They tried at the German border, no Austria-- Austrai border.

speaker: interviewee
text: When they were there, we went into  a family. We told him the whole story. We didn't tell him that we were Jewish. We told him that we liberated, and here is the border. We want to go to Switzerland. We want to go to-- to Canada. We didn't tell them to Israel. We wanted to go to Canada. He said, OK, Canada. And he was a little dealer wheeler.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, OK, my girl is working in Switzerland. Every day, every morning, they are going to Switzerland to work. In the evening, they are coming home. Just across the bridge, they are going to bring the tickets to St. Gallen. They were working over there. He says, OK. We stayed over there a few weeks. And all of a sudden, the French commander came and recognized me. I worked with him in Auschwitz. He said [? Gottlieb, ?] what are you doing here? He was the mayor from the little-- from a city near Paris. He said, what do you-- why don't you come to us in camp and live with us? So why do I want to go over there? I want to go to Israel. I'm not going over there. So he gave us packages. And he gave us some bikes. And we took that. And we ran around. And he says, where-- where are we going to go now. So we went back to  the family. We gave him this. And he brought us four tickets. But I can't swim. At night, we are going to swim. So we had to swim through to the train station not far like-- like [INAUDIBLE]-- like cross a street. So he said, don't worry. I arrange. He arranged. So we arranged, and we left him the horse. We left him the wagon [? before ?] we didn't need anything anymore. So she brought us the tickets, four tickets and brought us four-- four watches-- Tissot watches too, with the horse and wagon, with the packages with-- packages at that time was worth lots of money. There was like maybe 20 kilo in the package. There was cigarettes, sugar, coffee, tuna fish, everything. You mention it-- so milk-- powdered milk. So we-- the-- the train's supposed to go away to St. Gallen about 8:30. So we went to the fisherman. And the fisherman were there for us already with a little-- little boat. They took us over. We saw the train is not coming. He didn't know what to do. And all of a sudden, he heard train. So we go over there. And we made up with ourselves, we shouldn't be four-- all four together. It would be two and two if they catch us. But there was no police with-- with [INAUDIBLE] like here policemen you can recognize. No. So we were over there. And all of a sudden, the train comes. We got out of the train. We sit-- and I sit here with [? Rosenzweig, ?] I remember, I think [? with Rosenzweig ?] and my brother with another-- [? Morris Sandler. ?] What was your brother's name?

words.text: why do I want to go over there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Leon.

speaker: interviewee
text: So all of a sudden, the man comes. He said, where are we going? So suddenly, I said, we go to St. Gallen [INAUDIBLE]. Oh, so late? Yes. He talked perfect German too. So we got over there. It was late-- very late. We went to look a for a synagogue. We haven't got nobody to ask. That [? Sandler ?] was before the war in St. Gallen. And he had a map. And he know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we got in the synagogue. We knocked on the door. A Jewish fellow comes out. His name I can never forget in my life, Mr. [? Steinbeck. ?] And we got in. So we got in over there. When we got in over there, we told him that we just come. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We smuggled to the border. We just had come from Auschwitz. He didn't believe it. He thought maybe we were bandits. Who knows?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, listen, if that's the case, let's go upstairs. You want to sleep upstairs. Upstairs was a room-- a special room. You know, with-- you've got a room here. Every shul should have a room like that. They got a room like when you-- your book gets very old. The book getting old. So you have to have the-- then you have to take it and bury the book. You can't make a [? broker. ?] You have to bury it. I don't know if you know about it. You can't just throw it away or a bin. You have to bury it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we said, OK, and later [INAUDIBLE] he said let's go. I said, where are you gonna go? I opened the door,  I know the Jews sit over in synagogue with a beard. And I said, listen, I never saw-- I never saw a synagogue in so many years. Let's go. He said, don't worry. Let's go. We have a cup of tea. You know, in Switzerland, he says, custom. We have a cup of tea.

speaker: interviewee
text: As soon as we got out from the synagogue, first man inside was a detective. He said, listen, there is four fellows smuggled through. He arrested us right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Arrest us. Arrest us. So I said to him, listen, what have you done? He said, you can't here. [INAUDIBLE] you have to go legal. I said, what's the-- the-- the little things at the border? It was right on the border little things [INAUDIBLE]. There-- there were some people over there for the valuables that we sent through. So finally, what happened they put us in jail.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in jail. You would never believe it-- the same thing as Auschwitz to eat-- nothing. They gave us-- oh, you have nice, nice spoons-- nice-- nice forks, nice dishes-- very nice. To eat? Nothing, Judy. The first time in my life I had they gave you some bread-- for a piece. Mr. Silber, how long were you in-- in-- in jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: A week.

speaker: interviewer
text: A week. And what did you do after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me finish. So in the meantime, I said, we didn't want to eat. We wanted to go on a hunger strike. So the fellows from the jail called up the bank. His name was Guggenheim. He was the head like Rothschild here, like a-- no, like Bronfman here. He called up Guggenheim. Rabbi-- no, Guggenheim was a banker. And Guggenheim called up Rothschild. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So when they came, they guaranteed us, don't worry. [? We get-- ?] where do you want to go? We told him we wanted to go to Israel, because there was some passports. But the Germans fooled the Jews. He said-- they took the money away for numbers. The Jews had numbers. They called it [INAUDIBLE]. You have to know the number, because they put in the money and everything in Switzerland by the billions. Nobody knows how much money is in Switzerland.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know a few numbers. And I told him. He says, don't worry. We'll get you in deeper. We want to take you away from the border. That's all. And in a few days, we came. He bring us some oranges. I said, we don't want an orange. We don't want nothing. We want-- we want-- we want to go to Switzerland-- to Israel. He said, don't worry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all of a sudden, the guards come and took us over there. So I told to Mr. Rabbi Rothschild and to other rabbi, listen, tell us where are they taking us? They said, no, they take me to the bridge. And over there, they're gonna talk to the guard. So I was like, what has the guard got to do with it? If the-- if I want to [INAUDIBLE], has to come from the top. The guard can't do nothing. So I told them, listen, if they want to take us back, one of the two can run away. He hasn't got a gun. He has got nothing. At least two are gonna be here. He said, no, no. You can't do that in Switzerland. So no, no. He took us to the bridge. We stayed over there maybe half an hour, three-quarter of an hour and-- and listen-- nothing. [LAUGHS] Nothing. We-- Mr. Silber?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we-- from there you went to a-- a displaced person camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to go back where? I had to go back to the-- to the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I go back to the German and tell them the whole story. Listen, they catched us at the border. I didn't tell him that they're Jewish. They catched us at the border and didn't let us through. So that's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So and then-- the French found out again. So they come again. They gave us again packages and told us, you want to come to camp you're good, or not, no good, but you can't stay here. You're not allowed to stay at the border. So from there, we went back to Austria. Now, we were in-- You ended up in-- in a displaced persons camp in Austria?

words.text: you're good, or not, no good,

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Now, I went back to Innsbruck. In Innsbruck was very bombed. The hotel, everything was bombed. We had nothing  to sleep. So I found a fellow over there who I was with in Auschwitz and said, listen, you better stay with us at night. He stayed with us at night. You see tomorrow morning what's happening.

speaker: interviewee
text: And tomorrow morning, he comes to us. He says, listen, I heard that you got a cousin here. So where is my cousin? He's alive. You know. He went to Czechoslovakia. But I said-- excuse me, his sister got crazy. He went away to find out. So I waited. And he came back. And it was not my cousins from the same city. So he stayed over there. And there we stayed. And he stayed already over there, and that's it. So you stayed-- what was the name of the displaced person camp that you stayed?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] Gnadenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. From there, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] There, between-- I went back to Czechoslovakia, and I got married in Czechoslovakia. I didn't know my wife was alive. And I didn't know that [INAUDIBLE]. There was a fellow by the name [? Breem-- ?] [? Wushe ?] [? Breem. ?] He died in Philadelphia. He told me. Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: So the two of you-- so you married your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you came-- what happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: Came to Canada. In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: '48. To Canada '48. June the 15th, I arrived in Halifax.

speaker: interviewer
text: Halifax. And how many children do you have today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three.

speaker: interviewer
text: Three children. Very good. Can-- can I have the names of your children, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest is Allan. Then Anne-- I think you met Anne. Allan you met too, I guess. Shirley.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your-- and your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shirley, the youngest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very good. Can I ask you please why are you speaking out today? Why are you being-- giving testimony?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's late. Listen, if   I am not talking today, who knows what's gonna be happening with me tomorrow? It's finished anyway. [? It's over. ?] It's late. I haven't got time to wait.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what message do you have for your children, your grandchildren, and for your future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to say not to forget and not to forgive. That's what I would say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me how your family life was changed because of Shoa? How did your children-- do your children treat you differently than they would otherwise?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they always treat me good. They all are better-- a lot better with children. Very good. Not different at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your religious life-- how-- I know you were very religious when you were young. In-- in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not so readily. What can I lose, Judy? What can I lose? OK, go kill somebody? You're going to be better? Why should [? gripe ?] myself? Tell me what should I [? gripe? ?] Kill myself? Kill for a pair of shoes? Don't believe in nothing? You got to believe  in something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you are a very religious man.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't-- no, I wouldn't say that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you happy in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the best--

speaker: interviewer
text: Has it been a good life for you? I want to thank you--

speaker: interviewee
text: The best country in the world. God bless America. It couldn't be better. The best country. Really I didn't loss nothing in Canada. I wish my parents would be here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Silber, I want to thank you so very much for giving testimony today. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother. That's the Silber-- maiden name, [? Schuster. ?] This is my father Avram Silber. This is my sister, Sara Silber. This is my brother Zalick Silber. This is my brother [? Isaac ?] Silber. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is my-- my sister Bayla Silber. Her married name is [? Schaer. ?] This is my brother, Zalick. This is my brother Shmuel. He was an engineeer-- electrical engineer. This is Godel Silber. This is my sister Machla Silber. This is my brother David Silber. He died in [? Arizona. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where and when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is '29.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, Ciecanhow. I'm Godel Silber. And I'm-- I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are you sitting?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're sitting to the right on the bottom with a little dog. And this is my brother, Dave Silber. He survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is he sitting?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the left on the bottom two. And then survived, Bayla Silber-- [? Schaer. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is she?

speaker: interviewee
text: Between between my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Standing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Standing. This is Godel [? Schuster ?] to the right, my cousin-- [? Lily ?] [? Schuster ?] to the left from Israel. This is their child, [? Harvey ?] from Israel. This is my brother Dave Silber from Detroit. This is my sister, [? Fena ?] Silber from Detroit to the right. And this is [? Heldada ?] [? Esther ?] Silber from Detroit. And this is her husband. And this is my-- my brother's daughter from Detroit too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Gail ?] Silber.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: 17 years ago. This is my son, Allan Silber to  the right. This is my grandchild to the right. And this is Mr. Rosen. This is a Torah. And this is Godel Silber to the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your grandson's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Josh. This is my wife Cilka Silber-- maiden name [? Vecta. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to say anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could say now? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] What should I say? I'm happy to tell you about my husband. He is a wonderful person, and a wonderful husband, and a wonderful grandfather to our-- our whole family. And I hope to enjoy how much we have to-- all together.
